She is a goddess of hope, encouraging her followers to remain kind-hearted and empathetic even in the face of adversity.

Though she is gentle and merciful, she is also firm in her belief that healing requires balance. Her followers know that healing is not always about making someone “better,” but rather about helping them find strength and resilience, whether through recovery, acceptance, or passing peacefully.

docs.title: Domains and Powers:
docs.text:
• Healing and Restoration: Eir's primary domain is the healing of wounds, diseases, and ailments. She grants her followers the ability to cure diseases, heal injuries, and alleviate suffering. She can mend broken bones, heal fevers, and bring those on the brink of death back to life—although the restoration of life may require great sacrifice or a special ritual.

  • Mercy and Compassion: Eir embodies mercy in all its forms. She encourages acts of kindness, even toward enemies or those who have wronged others. Her followers often focus on forgiveness and helping others overcome their past mistakes, offering second chances where others might not.

  • Protection: Eir’s blessings often include protective spells or wards. Her presence in battle might manifest as a shield of divine light or a soothing aura that heals allies over time. Her followers are often called upon to safeguard those in need, offering shelter or defending the defenseless.

  • Balance of Life and Death: While Eir is a goddess of healing, she also understands the necessity of death in the cycle of life. Her followers understand that while they strive to heal, there are times when death must come, and that mercy lies in allowing the natural order to take its course. She is a goddess of compassion in both life and death, and her healing often includes helping individuals find peace in their final moments.

docs.title: Clergy and Followers:
docs.text:
Eir’s followers are often clerics, druids, or paladins who focus on the protection and care of others. They are healers, caretakers, and those who seek to alleviate suffering in the world. Many of her temples are built near places of natural beauty, such as tranquil forests, sacred springs, or peaceful groves, where the healing energy of nature is strongest.

The followers of Eir may serve as traveling medics, setting up healing stations in war-torn areas, or they may establish clinics or sanctuaries for those who are injured or ill. They often seek to foster peace and resolution, offering counsel to those in conflict and aiding in times of personal grief.

Eir's clergy wears robes of white or green, symbolizing her connection to nature and life. They carry healing tools such as bandages, potions, and salves, and are known for their skill in treating the sick and wounded.

docs.title: Symbolism and Items:
docs.text:
• Symbol: Eir's symbol is often a simple, intricate knot of life, representing the interconnectedness of all things and the balance between healing and death. Some depictions show a staff entwined with healing herbs or a sacred chalice with a healing potion.

  • Sacred Items: Eir’s sacred item might be an ornate vial filled with a powerful healing elixir, capable of healing any injury or sickness when used correctly. It’s said that when this elixir is poured onto the earth, it can heal the land itself, bringing fertility and life where there was once death or decay.

docs.title: Role in a Campaign:
docs.text:
Eir could be a patron deity for any character with a focus on healing, compassion, or protection. A cleric or paladin devoted to Eir would be able to channel divine energy to heal wounds, cure diseases, and protect their allies. Eir's influence could manifest as a calming presence in tense situations, as her followers work to bring about peace or prevent unnecessary violence.

Eir could also serve as an important deity where restoration and mercy are key themes. Her followers may seek out rare herbs or lost healing knowledge to aid a suffering village, or perhaps the party must retrieve a powerful artifact of healing from a dangerous location, with Eir guiding them along the way.

Her followers might be called to mediate disputes between warring factions or heal the victims of a terrible plague. Alternatively, the campaign could focus on a powerful artifact or healing magic lost to time, and Eir’s wisdom could be crucial to understanding its true purpose.

docs.title: Interacting with Eir:
docs.text:
Eir’s blessings might manifest in subtle ways, such as a sudden surge of strength when an ally is on the brink of death or a burst of warmth that soothes the pain of a grievous wound. If a character prays or makes a sacrifice to Eir, they may experience a moment of profound clarity, or perhaps a gentle whisper in the wind that guides them toward their next healing task.

A campaign centered around Eir might involve themes of self-sacrifice, mercy, and the value of healing—not just physical wounds, but emotional and spiritual ones as well. She could challenge the party to save an entire town from a deadly disease or aid a fallen hero in finding peace as they pass into the afterlife.

docs.title: Ullr
docs.text:
Ullr is a powerful and revered Norse god often associated with hunting, archery, winter, and the outdoors. Ullr is a god who embodies both the serene beauty and the harsh, unforgiving elements of nature. He is a god of skill, precision, and survival, known for his keen eye and unmatched prowess with a bow.

Appearance: Ullr may be portrayed as a tall, imposing figure, with long, blonde hair and piercing blue eyes. His features are sharp, like the edge of an arrowhead, and his body is well-toned and lean from years of training in the wilderness. He often wears furs and light armor that allows him to move freely through snow and ice. In combat, he wields a great bow made from the wood of ancient trees, with arrows that never miss their mark.

Personality: Ullr is both disciplined and wise. He appreciates strength and resilience, but also values patience and strategy. He is not a god of chaos or mindless destruction but rather one of calculated precision and skill. His followers, often hunters or rangers, believe that success is not just about power but about the right timing and the right tools. He is stoic, rarely speaking unless necessary, but when he does, his words carry great weight. He is not quick to anger, but when offended, his vengeance is swift and deadly.

Domains and Powers:

  • Archery and Hunting: Ullr is the god of hunters, providing his followers with perfect aim and the ability to track creatures across difficult terrain. Those who seek to improve their archery skills or become legendary hunters often pray to Ullr.
  • Winter and Survival: Ullr's domain includes the winter elements—snow, ice, and cold winds. He is revered by those who venture into harsh climates and need to survive. His power may be called upon to endure harsh conditions or to ensure a successful hunt during the winter months.
  • Protection of the Wild: Ullr also has a protective side for the natural world, ensuring that the balance between hunter and prey is maintained, and that the wilderness remains untamed and beautiful.

Clergy and Followers: Priests of Ullr often live as nomads or in isolated mountain temples. They are masters of survival in the harshest environments and are trained in archery and hunting. His followers include rangers, hunters, and scouts, but also those who seek solitude or wisdom in nature. His temples are often constructed in remote, snow-capped mountains or deep forests, where the trials of the wilderness are an integral part of their daily lives.

Symbolism and Items:

  • Symbol: Ullr's symbol is a crossed bow and arrow, often framed by snowflakes or a wreath of evergreen leaves.
  • Sacred Item: His favored weapon, a longbow made of ancient wood, is said to possess incredible accuracy, capable of striking down even the most elusive of creatures. Some legends suggest it can also summon the chill winds of winter when drawn in battle.

Ullr can be a patron for a ranger, a hunter, or a barbarian who is at home in the cold and the wild. He might also serve as an antagonist in a campaign where the balance of nature is disturbed, with his followers seeking to restore it. His influence could be felt in a cold, dangerous wilderness area, where the party must prove their worth to gain his favor or defeat a cult that follows his darker, more vengeful aspect.

In combat, invoking Ullr might grant the party heightened accuracy with ranged weapons or the ability to track enemies even through blizzards. Alternatively, the party might need to appease Ullr's wrath, facing challenges in the snow or battling creatures twisted by his frigid domain.

docs.title: Dwarves
docs.text:
AKA Dark-Elves

In Norse mythos dwarves are magical crafters of huge renown and notable traders, though their societies are mostly introverted and don't allow visitors.

docs.title: Elves
docs.text:
The Elves were the first children of the gods. Beloved by the gods and given many gifts, the Elves raised up great cities and conquered the wild untamed lands of Valkair by protecting themselves with harnessed magical power and great walls. The Elves were an experiment somewhere between the immortality of the gods and the frailty of the Valkair peoples.

Despite their accomplishments for some reason the Elves have been dwindling of late. Perhaps this is the prevalence of the barbaric races and the interbreeding between them or perhaps the Elves themselves are responsible for their own downfall.

The Elves are a strict class system and selective monarchy where the High King/Queen is selected from the ruling aristocrazy of the noble families, due to their long lifespans the selection of a new ruler is rarely needed.

  • Thrandurassil Elves
  • Netherese Elves
  • [[Finarfin]] Elves
  • [[Earendil]] Elves
  • [[Idril]] Elves

The Elves suffer no dilution of the elven bloodline and mixing elven blood with any other species is treated as blasphemy against the Gods and one of the highest crimes of Elven society. Any child born of mixed blood is cast out and forfeits all birthrights.

This behavior parallels their treatment of the "barbaric races" which is to only suffer them temporarily in times of trade.

docs.title: Humans
docs.text:
Based on Vikings

  • Norse humans of The Middens

docs.title: Ork
docs.text:
Horde

Orks are a formidable and resilient species, characterized by their large, muscular builds and distinctive features. They typically have tough, green or grayish skin, with prominent tusks that protrude from their lower jaws, adding to their imposing appearance. Many Orks wear their hair long, often braided and adorned with beads or ornaments that symbolize their clan or personal achievements. Their clothing is practical, made from furs, leather, and metal, reflecting a rugged lifestyle suited for their harsh environments.

Ork society is organized into tight-knit clans, each governed by a chieftain or leader chosen for their strength and leadership abilities. Honor, loyalty, and strength are highly valued traits, and the bonds of kinship and community are fundamental to their culture. Celebrations of shared victories and achievements are common, with feasting and storytelling serving as vital components of their social life. Oral traditions play an essential role in preserving the history of their people, with bards recounting tales of legendary warriors and heroic deeds.

Combat is a central aspect of Orkish life, with warriors trained from a young age in various forms of combat. They are known for their brute strength and tactical ingenuity in battle, wielding weapons like axes, swords, and bows with great skill. Orks view warfare as a means of achieving honor and glory, believing that those who die bravely in battle are rewarded in Valhalla.

Spirituality is deeply ingrained in Orkish culture, characterized by a profound connection to nature and reverence for ancestral spirits. Central to their beliefs is the worship of powerful deities drawn from a pantheon reminiscent of the Norse gods.

[[Thor]], the god of thunder and war, holds a special place in the hearts of the Orks. They admire his strength and valor in battle, often invoking his name before embarking on raids or significant confrontations. Orkish warriors pray to Thor for courage and protection, believing that his fierce spirit guides them in combat, helping them to achieve glory on the battlefield. Rituals may include offerings of weapons or symbolic tokens, which are believed to honor Thor and earn his favor in the heat of battle.

[[Freya]], the goddess of love, war, and death, is another significant figure in their spiritual practices. Orks honor Freya for her dual nature, recognizing that she embodies both the nurturing aspects of life and the fierce qualities of a warrior. They seek her blessings for both fertility and success in battle, reflecting their understanding of the interconnectedness of creation and destruction. Festivals dedicated to Freya are marked by celebrations of both love and strength, where warriors are honored, and tales of valor are recounted alongside prayers for prosperity and protection.

Shamans serve as spiritual leaders within Orkish communities, conducting rituals that honor both the natural world and their forebears. They engage in ceremonies to commune with the spirits of the land and the ancestors, ensuring that the lessons and stories of the past are preserved. These practices emphasize harmony with the earth and the importance of respecting the cycle of life and death. Orks believe that their ancestors watch over them, guiding their actions and offering protection through the trials of life.

As a species, Orks embody a rich tapestry of values centered around strength, honor, and community. Their fierce warrior spirit, deep-rooted traditions, and profound connection to nature and their ancestors make them a compelling and respected presence in Valkair.

docs.title: Major Language Groups and Dialects
docs.text:
The ork's tend to exaggerate and drag the first word or the beginning of the first words or phrases as they start talking. Usually the vowel or any initial sound might be dragged. They also speak slowly at first and have a harsher and thicker voice. They tend to prefer and use simple language and may not have a high range of vocabulary as common is not their first language. Consider examples:

Taaaake his head off!

Wheeeeeere are you from?

Youuuuu are a fancy one.

Weeeee found him hiding in the gatehouse. Aaaaafter a few punches and me ripping his ear off. He told me where his friends are. Aaaaalso said some other words but I have not mated with any mothers recently so that does not matter I think.

docs.title: Smallfolk
docs.text:
Collective name for the small peoples, this includes

  • Halflings
  • Gnomes
  • Goblins
  • Ratfolk

docs.title: 1.1.0 - Introduction: Hammerfall Catacombs
docs.text:
You came to Hammerfall as nameless soldiers in a horde, and you went into the dark beneath it because the men in black robes said there was another way in.

The fortress on the river had never been taken. You watched it prove why. The first boats of the Great Barbarian Army surged forward with a warcry, Norse and Ork rowing side by side for once in their lives, and an outer tower opened up with elemental fire. The front line burned and splintered and sank, and the oars of the boats behind hung still over the water. The commanders screamed for the assault to continue. The boats that obeyed met the same end. Then new orders came down the line.

A scattering of vessels broke south up a tributary, following a lead boat crewed by figures in black robes that fell to within inches of the deck. The Chosen, the man at the oar beside you called them. Nobody had seen what was under the cloth. They said they knew another road in. The river narrowed until you carried the boats by hand, and the Chosen stopped you at a wet black gap in the rock and told you to go down.

You were soldiers, and soldiers are spent. When one of you fell in the dark, the next one down the hole picked up where they left off. One such body wandered into a side chamber it had been warned away from and learned what the Chosen keep under their robes. Deformed things with too many limbs came out of the black and tore the party apart. Whoever saw it did not live to carry it back to the army.

Deeper in you found the kobolds of the Scree-Scree-Rah, barricaded behind their own rubbish and rigged noise-traps, braced for the Chosen who keep coming to drag them off for sacrifice. Talking failed. The fight was long and ugly and ended with the kobold king dead and the rest on their knees. You took their mage prisoner, and he proved more useful alive. He knew the way up toward the elven city, and he untangled the puzzle of the three columns and the one-eyed mural that none of you had cracked. Through the wall it hid, you came on elves at last, guards at their ease and workers tending their dead, none of them expecting a fight. You were out of everything and people dropped fast. It came down to Hanzt, alone, standing over the last guard as it fell. The workers raised their hands.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Who you are: nameless soldiers of the Great Barbarian Army, the Norse tribes and an Ork horde joined by one thing, their hatred of the elves. Fall, and the next soldier down the hole takes your place.
  • The Chosen: black-robed guides who promised another way into Hammerfall. Under the robes they are deformed, monstrous things. Your characters do not know this yet. You do.
  • The kobold prisoner: the Scree-Scree-Rah mage you spared, now your guide toward the elven city.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Through the elven burial chamber and climbing toward Upper Hammerfall, low on resources, a captive kobold leading the way.

docs.title: 1.2.0 - A city overrun
docs.text:
A voice in a ruby asked for help, you broke her loose, and then you climbed out into a city where every living soul had simply vanished.

Down in the catacombs the voice would not let you rest. A woman's voice, soft and far away, asking if anyone could hear her, telling you she was trapped and needed help. It came from a portal of something like water, an elven device with a ruby the size of a fist throbbing at its peak. Some of you stepped through to where she was held: a girl who did not know her own mother or father, only that she had once been a farmer's daughter. Freeing her was simple and violent. You prised the ruby from its setting and it burst to red dust, and a shockwave of raw magic threw you off your feet and tore outward through the stone. A soft voice followed it, like an aftershock. Thank you.

A second mural of Odin waited on the way out, this one with both eyes where the first had only one. Count them true, the elvish read, something false but out of view. You pressed his right eye, it crumbled to a hidden ring, and the door swung open on an elven vault.

Then the long climb, and Upper Hammerfall at the top of it. Goblets half full on the tables. Meals laid out and left to rot. Quills resting in inkwells above letters that would never be finished, a harp leaning against a chair with no hand to play it. No footprints in the dust. Not a city emptied so much as a city where everyone had been plucked out of the world between one breath and the next.

Then ten years passed. Hammerfall became someone else's city, and the heroes of that day became four down-and-out strangers nursing thin stew at the Black Tide Tavern, under the not-quite-motherly eye of Varka, called Low Tide. Brawn, running from the law and wanting a clean start. Riven, still hunting the friends he lost. Gunnar, a bounty hunter back to see old Balder. Hanzt, returned wearing a very different face. And Balder behind the bar, drinking down the wages he needed for drink. Balder had a job going, passed down from the Rat King of Loki's Hand. Easy work. Take a rowboat out, meet a delivery, escort it safely into the city. Nothing to it.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The girl in the portal: you freed a trapped soul who knew nothing of where she came from. The ruby that held her is dust now. She said thank you.
  • Empty Upper Hammerfall: the elves did not flee. They vanished mid-motion, all at once, and left a perfect, untouched city behind.
  • Ten years on: you are scattered, broke, and reunited at the Black Tide Tavern under Varka. Balder has lined you up a smuggling job for Loki's Hand.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: A loaned rowboat on the river north of Hammerfall, ten years older and short on coin, rowing out to meet smugglers for the Rat King.

docs.title: 1.2.1 - A toe in line
docs.text:
A simple smuggling run, until the things waiting in the cave turned out to be carrying Guild coin.

The job was meant to be easy. Row out north of Hammerfall to where the river bends, meet the smugglers, take their packages into the Undercroft. Don't open them, don't break them, or Kvarrac would have everyone's heads. The smugglers showed you a cave mouth hidden behind hedge and riverweed, handed off the goods, and sailed away before you could ask a single useful question.

The cave was a world of its own. A forest of mushrooms, an abandoned shrine, a murky lake. You chose kindness where you could, befriending the creatures down there, the Blindheim and the Naiad both, and healing what ailed them. The Naiad pressed a water blessing onto Hanzt, its purpose still a mystery.

Then the wererats came, and the easy job stopped being easy. They knew too much about your delivery. When one of them fell you found why that should not have been possible: a Guild coin in its pocket, and a note that read take care of our little problem. This smuggling ring was supposed to be invisible to the Guild. It was not. You carried the cockatrice eggs the rest of the way to the Undercroft, met Varka properly, and unloaded the goods. For your trouble you had a roof, free lodging at the Black Tide while you ran with Loki's Hand, and a new and unwelcome question. How did the Guild already know?

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The Guild knows: a dead wererat carried a Guild coin and orders to "take care of our little problem." Your hidden job was not hidden. Hanzt is convinced the Guild is hunting him in particular.
  • Hanzt's water blessing: a gift from the Naiad, effect still unknown.
  • Lodging at the Black Tide: Varka is putting you up while you work for Loki's Hand.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Back at the Black Tide Tavern with free beds and a bad feeling, freshly in the service of Loki's Hand.

docs.title: 1.2.2 - A friend in need
docs.text:
The Rat King made you family, and the family's first job was a missing elf in a district where elves are left to starve.

A week of idleness ended with a fist on the door and a summons to the back room. Varka was drinking before noon and looked worried about it. Beside her stood Kvarrac, the Rat King himself: aged, spry, a hairless tail curling in the candlelight, sharp teeth under the hood. He wanted oaths. No backward steps, no leaving the family. He embraced each of you and named you Wolves of Loki's Hand. Two others drifted in without a sound. Brelka, called The Riptide, all clipped words and itching to be elsewhere, who thought throwing green Wolves at a job this hot was exactly the kind of stupid Kvarrac specialised in, and said so before storming out. And Grettir, The Leech, a scarred man with bone saws on his belt and a promise: you will lose someone eventually, and that is what he is for, if you have the coin.

The job: Veylith Lorarion, Loki's Hand's one elven contact, the man who moves food to the starving in exchange for old elven secrets, had gone missing. An elite Ork detachment called the Uzzikh was tangled up in it. Get him back, quietly, and never lead anyone home.

So you walked into the Eastern Elf Shallows and saw what the Pact allows there. Elven children too weak to wrestle an Ork child for a mouldy loaf. A bell tolling, and a mother and father carrying a wrapped bundle the size of a small elf toward an ancient tomb the locals swear opens onto Hel. At the crumbling Temple of Heimdall the one-eyed priest Varthan Greycloak told you what little he had. The first to vanish was not Veylith but an Ork named Rashan, a guard unusually gentle with the elves, which made her a strange one to disappear when so many crueller Orks never did. You found the Uzzikh's warehouse down in the dark of the docks, lit from within, and went quiet.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• You are Wolves now: sworn into Loki's Hand by Kvarrac, no leaving the family. Brelka (The Riptide) thinks you will get everyone killed; Grettir (The Leech) will stitch you back together for coin.
  • The job: rescue Veylith Lorarion, the elf who trades food for elven secrets.
  • Rashan: the missing Ork who set all this off, notable only for being kind to elves. The Ancient Elven Tomb is said to be a doorway to Hel, and back to the catacombs.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Outside the Uzzikh's warehouse in the Mongeet docks, ready to move on it quietly.

docs.title: 1.2.3 - What remains
docs.text:
You opened the warehouse door, and the smell told you what the Uzzikh had been doing before your eyes could.

The smell reached you first. Death, old blood and bone, and then the sight of it: elven bodies heaped on the far side, the freshest of them flayed and roped to tables and crates, limp and unconscious. From a closed office came light and screaming. An interrogation still underway. The Uzzikh had been at this for two days, killing elves for scraps about the missing Rashan, and now they had Veylith.

In the office was Bloodsnout, an ork in red and purple command leathers, tusks jutting from his lower jaw, a bat wound in barbed wire bleeding onto the floor at his feet. He did not believe he was doing anything wrong, and he honestly did not know why you had come. There was a version of this where words might have worked. This was not that version. The fight broke fast and ugly. Three of his warriors went down breathing. Five did not, Bloodsnout among them. You took his barbed bat, Vorgra, off his corpse and cut Veylith loose.

You walked out harder than you walked in, with a rescued elf and a building full of the Pact's quiet cruelty left behind you.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Veylith is free: Loki's Hand's elven contact, pulled out of Bloodsnout's interrogation alive.
  • Bloodsnout dead: you claimed his barbed-wire bat, Vorgra, and left five Uzzikh dead, three unconscious.
  • Rashan still missing: the Uzzikh tortured and killed elves for two days over her and learned nothing. She is still gone.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Leaving the Uzzikh warehouse with Veylith rescued, the Eastern Shallows no safer than you found it.

docs.title: 1.2.4 - Over claw and tide
docs.text:
You came back from saving the elves to find the Hand eating itself, and Brelka holding the knife.

You torched the Uzzikh warehouse behind you and slipped through the alleys with Veylith and the freed elves pressed close, the whole way feeling watched and never finding the eyes. You hid them in a cellar built by old elven hands beneath a tailor's shop, the Tattered Loom, in the Western Elf Shallows. Veylith looked at you with exhaustion and gratitude both, and said he would remember what you did for his people.

Then back to the Black Tide, and something wrong in the air of it. Too many bodies, too late at night, all of them awake and alert and watching the door. Even Varka had noticed. It was not right, she muttered, nobody here to drink, maybe a new drug on her streets she needed to trace.

The source walked into the backroom. Brelka and Grettir, two of those jittery patrons at their backs, twitching with the same restless energy Brelka always carried. She held the room like a blade. She reminded Kvarrac of the family the three of them had built from nothing in the smuggling caves, then named him soft, a killer gone caretaker for orphans and elves. She told him that when food runs short, rats eat each other. Then she put her dagger into his chest, again and again, and her body broke open into matted fur and claws and a long rodent snarl. Her companions changed with her. The walls and floor split apart, and the rats came pouring through. You fought for the tavern in the wreckage of it. Grettir went down, Varka herself accounting for him and a heap of the vermin besides. Brelka slipped away into the dark.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Kvarrac is dead: Brelka, The Riptide, murdered the Rat King and revealed herself a wererat. She turned the Hand against itself and fled.
  • Grettir is dead: killed defending the Tide. Varka was a terror in that fight.
  • Veylith is safe: hidden with the rescued elves beneath the Tattered Loom. He owes you, and he knows it.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Standing in the blood and splinters of the Black Tide Tavern. Kvarrac dead, Brelka loose, the Hand in pieces.

docs.title: 1.2.5 - The Undercroft
docs.text:
Varka opened a trapdoor under the floor of her ruined tavern and sent you down it to finish Brelka for good.

Varka stood over the carnage with a bloody cutlass and white-hot rage, naming Brelka every filthy thing she could reach for. Half her regulars dead. Kvarrac gutted. She knew where the coward had run: down into the Undercroft, to hide and to recruit more cursed things to her side. She kicked aside a barrel, sank her axe through the floorboards onto a hollow thud, and hauled up the trapdoor. She would stay above to stitch the wounded, drop the dead off the pier, and keep mouths shut. You would go down and cut the rot out. Do that, she said, and you would rebuild the Hand together, maybe even into the thing Kvarrac always talked about.

Down you went. Rats first, caught feeding on a corpse at an overlook. Then a rest that did not stay restful, the watch slipping just as carrion crawlers came up out of the dark. You put them down without anyone going stiff, and only then thought to search the body the rats had been eating. Hanzt turned up a small cache of potions and named them on sight, and somewhere in the picking-over he found he could learn formulas and recipes from what he scavenged. You healed up overlooking the cave, the hunt still ahead.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The hunt is sanctioned: Varka has sent you into the Undercroft to kill Brelka and end the threat. Finish it, and you help rebuild Loki's Hand.
  • Hanzt's craft: he can now learn alchemical formulas and recipes from what he scavenges.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Rested at the lip of the Undercroft caves, hunting Brelka into the dark.

docs.title: 1.2.6 - Ratmother
docs.text:
Brelka's people had turned the caves into a trap, and Grettir had left one last monster behind, waiting for a word.

The main cavern roared with falling water that swallowed every other sound. Brelka's wererats had been busy. They had torn down the ladder from the overlook, seeded the moss at the foot of the cliffs with hidden spikes, and set a camp to wait for anyone fool enough to climb down. You came through it anyway.

Deeper sat Grettir's last work: a carrion golem, a thing stitched from rot and dead flesh, finished and standing dormant, its research book open on a lectern beside it. The book held the command word that would wake it. With Grettir dead it should have stayed asleep. It did not stay asleep, because Hanzt read the word aloud, and the golem came up swinging, mindless, powered by the soul gem buried in its chest. You brought it down and dug the gem out of the reeking corpse, its throb slowing to a soft, steady glow.

You did not walk away clean. The fight left its marks under the skin. Gunnar and Brawn both carry the wererat's curse now, and three of you climbed out of the muck with filth fever setting in.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The soul gem: prised from Grettir's carrion golem, no longer pulsing, its purpose still unknown.
  • The curse spreads: Gunnar and Brawn are infected with the wererat curse. Gunnar, Brawn, and Hanzt all caught filth fever.
  • Hanzt and command words: perhaps do not read the strange book aloud next time.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Past the wererat traps and Grettir's golem, deeper in the Undercroft, sick and cursed but still on Brelka's trail.

docs.title: 1.2.7 - Gods have mercy
docs.text:
Through a hole in the wall you heard who Brelka really answers to, and his voice was worse than hers.

You tore through a rough breach in the stone, two hours of work, and from the far side came voices. A man and Brelka, mid-argument. She was wild with grief, screaming that her friends were dead, that he had wanted them dead, that all of this was what he wanted. Tarod. His reply came slow and cutting: how disappointing she was, when he had thought she might rise above her weakness and find a place in their order, and how fitting that this ruined temple should suit her failure. Prove yourself, he told her. If you survive, perhaps you will be worth my time. Then he split the air with a crack of power and was gone into black fog.

Beyond the wall lay Loki's temple. You found Brelka's journal, the early pages full of raids run with Varka, the later ones decaying into mad scribble. You freed Sigurd, locked up for refusing to become a wererat, and sent him back toward the Black Tide. He told you of two temples down here, one to Heimdall and one to Loki the trickster, where Brelka had been spending most of her time of late. And in cages you found rats that were wrong, bulging and stretched and manic, Grettir's handiwork, primed to burst.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Tarod: the cold voice behind Brelka, of some "order," who wanted the Hand's people dead and discards Brelka like a spent tool. Remember the name.
  • Sigurd freed: imprisoned for refusing the curse, now headed back to the Black Tide.
  • Two temples below: one to Heimdall, one to Loki the trickster, where Brelka lingers. And caged explosive rats, courtesy of Grettir.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: At the threshold of Loki's temple in the Undercroft, Brelka cornered somewhere ahead and her master's name fresh in your ears.

docs.title: 1.2.8 - Loki's Kiss
docs.text:
You ran Brelka to ground in Loki's temple, and Gunnar walked out wearing a god's ring he could not take off.

Loki's temple opened out so vast and dark it made you small. Torches guttered against a void of a ceiling. Brelka was already there, crouched among giant vases and a circular altar still wet and red, hugging her knees and muttering. She had done what was required. She would raise them higher than Kvarrac ever dreamed, if only he had understood. Then she saw you, called you pups, and said what a shame it was that you had to die too. Her bones cracked and stretched, fur forced through her skin, her teeth doubled, and a long hairless tail slammed down behind her. She came at you hungry, and you put her down for good on the cold stone.

In the chest behind the altar, under a mural of Loki's gluttonous feast, you found the haul: a striking rune, ghost-touched gloves, boots of striding, a skeleton key, and a steel ring set with a small recess shaped like a pair of lips. Loki's Kiss. Gunnar slid it on and gave the god a cheeky kiss back. Everyone walked away a level stronger. And somewhere under all of it, though most of you could not yet say how, every one of you is tangled up with the name Tarod.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Brelka is dead: the hunt is over. Loki's Hand can be rebuilt now, or buried with her.
  • Loki's Kiss: Gunnar took the lips-marked ring and put it on. That may prove to be a decision rather than a trinket.
  • Tarod: each of you has a thread tying you to him, even if you cannot all see it yet.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Standing over Brelka's corpse in Loki's temple, richer by a chest of treasure and one questionable ring.

docs.title: 1.3.1 - Of the blood
docs.text:
Brelka was dead and avenged, and then Gunnar reached up to take the ring off and learned that Loki does not give his gifts back.

Brelka lay shattered against the stone of Loki's temple. The vengeance was done, Varka's and yours both, and what came next for the Hand was anyone's guess. You spat on her corpse and caught the gleam of the ring on your finger, the one that had fallen from hers. It clung now like it belonged. Gunnar reached to pull it off, and pain swallowed him whole: Loki's warm embrace, a hand on his face, lips meeting the god's, a tongue winding around his own. Every tug felt like tearing his own tongue out by the root. The ring stays. Whatever it is, Gunnar wears it now.

You went back through the traps you had skipped, half of them already disabled without your noticing, and climbed up out of Loki's temple. A spiral stair led to a chamber of stone doors marked with eyes. Inside waited goblins and a Barghest grown fat on elven dead, and you cut them all down, but not for free. Something in that fight left Riven with a wound that will not close. Ordinary care tears open again within moments; only magic holds the rot at bay, and each dawn he gets one more chance to throw it off. All around lay elves, torn apart and heaped in the dark.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Loki's Kiss is permanent: Gunnar cannot remove the ring. Trying brings a vision of Loki and real agony. He is bound to the trickster now.
  • Riven's wound: a curse from the Barghest. It will not close on its own and resists all but magical healing.
  • The eyed doors: a chamber above Loki's temple, marked with eyes, full of slaughtered elves.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Climbing up through the catacombs past the eyed doors, Gunnar ringed to a god and Riven carrying a wound that will not heal.

docs.title: 1.3.2 - Scratching in the dark
docs.text:
You cleared a god's treasure out of the dark, and then six elite Orks walked into the middle of your massacre and asked who you were.

You were wounded and filthy and standing in a slaughterhouse, elves heaped and torn, some of them killed slow while they still breathed. A great column nearby leaked thin shafts of light into the black. Behind one of the eyed doors you found and killed a lurking spider, and past it Heimdall's chest: a lens, a blessed lightstone, a horn. Then torchlight came down the tunnel, boots in disciplined rhythm, and six Orks in real forged plate marked with clan glyphs, escorted by the silent Chosen. At their head, Grukk One-Tusk, one tusk snapped halfway down, eyes sharp with years of judgment.

He found you bloodied in the middle of a massacre on sealed Chosen ground, and he wanted answers before he dragged you up in chains. You gave them straight. He looked at Riven's cursed wound and the dead and decided you had crawled through the same hell these poor bastards died in rather than made it. He pointed you to the stair up into the Eastern Shallows and told you not to come back down unless you were Chosen, with a sour glance at the robed escort he was stuck sharing these tombs with. Then a week to lick your wounds, runes shuffled between you, and home to the Black Tide, where Varka and Balder poured drinks and told the old stories of Kvarrac and Brelka and how the Hand began. Whatever you were now, Varka said, you would always be welcome at the Tide. Hanzt, for his part, still could not shake his fever.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Grukk One-Tusk: an Ork elite who judged you innocent of the elven killings and let you walk, warning you off the catacombs. Worth remembering as more than an enemy.
  • Heimdall's chest: you carried off Heimdall's Lens, a Blessed Lightstone, and the Sentinel Horn.
  • Hanzt's fever: still uncured and worsening, even after treatment.
  • The Tide is home: Varka named you welcome there for good.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Back at the Black Tide Tavern after a week's rest, patched up and resupplied, the catacombs behind you for now.

docs.title: 1.4.01 - Midsommerblót
docs.text:
Varka sent you to catch a god's beast alive, and at the last moment someone else put an arrow in it.

With Loki's Hand broken and the poorer Shallows looking for someone to follow, Varka pointed you at the one thing that buys a name in Hammerfall: the Midsommerblót, the sacred hunt. Every solstice a spirit called Hrimgaldr settles into some worthy beast in the Lost Woods, and whoever brings it back alive for the sacrifice is sung across the city and the villages beyond. Bring it back dead, and the year sours into famine and the gods' silence. Free drink for a month, Varka added, and everyone forgets the rats.

Into the misty, thorned woods you went. Gunnar called on Loki for the way, pouring wine over the ring and sealing it with a kiss, and the trickster murmured to press on where there seemed to be no path. Riven read the hidden trails like the forest remembered him. You found a dryad sick with rot and chose to help her, promising to cleanse her and her tree, and left her well-disposed toward you. Hanzt bathed in the healing pool of the Sacred Grove and felt the filth fever loosen its grip at last. Then you cornered Hrimgaldr, antlered and iron-furred and ancient, and very nearly calmed him.

And then the orcs came out of the trees and killed him. Arrows in the great beast, his breath going shallow against the ground, the hunt broken by another's hand. Whatever the gods made of that, it was no longer yours to give.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The hunt failed: you nearly took Hrimgaldr alive, but orc hunters killed him first. A slain spirit-beast is said to curse the year with famine and the gods' disfavour.
  • Loki guides Gunnar: the ring answers. Wine and a kiss, and the trickster shows the way.
  • Small mercies: the dryad of the Sacred Grove owes you goodwill, and the Grove's waters eased Hanzt's fever.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Standing over a dead god's beast in the Lost Woods, the hunt lost to another's arrows, the sacrifice still to come.

docs.title: 1.4.02 - Eyes of the Gods
docs.text:
The boar bled out on the altar, and in the blood-haze each of you saw something the gods wanted you to see.

The hunt had ended wrong, but the rite went on. They carried Hrimgaldr to the Godtooth, the old stone circle on the cold shoulder of the mountain, and the Speaker of the Chosen spilled his blood across the stone. Mead and chanting and the iron reek of it filled your heads, and one by one the gods showed you things.

Hanzt walked back into the Guild's chambers: a choice of who to torture, a plague he could cure for the price of corruption in his own blood, a golden throne room where a dwarf king offered him his old rank if he would only put a blade in Tarod. He did mostly as he was bid. Brawn saw the weight of glory, cheered as a hero of Murgrog over a weeping orc mother, his home in flames, his family dead with his brother's voice saying you saved them all, just not us, and a pit fight where stolen gloves fed on his violence until a boy knelt over a ruined body and looked up at him with hate. Brawn tried to hold to mercy through all of it. Riven chased Liora, Torran, and Etta through a summer that turned cold, his lost friends grown haggard and accusing, and then the trapped girl bleeding on the altar again, taking his amulet, her eyes opening wide and wrong while a voice that was not hers said good boy, you always choose wrong. Riven could not stop himself from helping.

You came back to the cold of the peak with the visions still clinging to you, none of them explained, all of them heavy.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The visions: the gods showed each of you your own wound. Hanzt and the Guild. Brawn and the cost of glory, and Murgrog. Riven and his lost friends, and the girl who takes his amulet.
  • A warning in the dark: Riven's vision tied his amulet, and that trapped girl, to something waking and wrong.
  • Gunnar's turn: the gods were not finished. His vision had not yet come.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: On the Godtooth peak as the blood-haze clears, marked by visions none of you yet understand.

docs.title: 1.4.03 - A strange visitor
docs.text:
Gunnar finally saw where he came from, and then a god kicked open the tavern door and told you his daughter needed saving.

All of Hammerfall had climbed to the Godtooth for the sacrifice, the Pact at the head of it, the Speaker and Ivar and Orgrim and the trader Yennen, and all of them learned your names. Then the last vision came, and it was Gunnar's. He stood in a runed tomb and heard a baby scream with his own voice. An elven woman, his mother, held the bundled newborn out to robed Guild figures who promised her a place among them if she would only use the knife. She raised it. She hesitated. Then she turned and threw it into a Guild throat, pressed a mirror charm to the baby's chest, screamed to Odin to let the child be seen, and opened her own chest across the stone in a blast of divine fire. The rest came in fragments. The Pack burning elven homes, his mother run down in the trees, the orcs later finding a half-blood infant still breathing under a faintly glowing charm and deciding to keep him. And Loki, lounging in mirrors and wine, naming him a bastard of gods and monsters and asking which side he would choose.

Then down the mountain to the Black Tide, where the feast was already roaring, and the doors slammed open on a god. Heimdall himself, booming and wrapped in furs, leapt onto a table and demanded stories and challenges. There were boasts and there was drink. Gunnar bragged on Riven's aim, and Heimdall threw an apple, and Riven pinned it to the wall to a roar from the hall. Gunnar admitted out loud that he is half elf. And between the games Heimdall told you the thing that mattered. The girl you freed from the portal is his daughter, Hannah, born to the High Queen Aduni, a child he has never met. He does not know what became of her, only that she vanished and then returned, and he begged you to save her. Beneath the city, he said, run eight temples, one for each old god, deep as the Shallows are wide. Answers might be down there. You have already stood in two of them.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Gunnar's blood: son of an elven mother who died to hide him from the Guild and call Odin's eye to him, and of an orc of The Pack who raided her people. Loki keeps asking which half he will be.
  • Hannah: the girl from the portal is Heimdall's daughter by the High Queen Aduni. Heimdall has charged you to save her.
  • Eight temples: one for each old god, hidden beneath the city. You have been in two. The rest may hold answers about Hannah.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Drinking with a god at the Black Tide, newly charged by Heimdall to find and save his lost daughter, Hannah.

docs.title: 1.4.04 - Siraen Valar
docs.text:
The elves the city forgot at the feast answered the next morning with fire carved into their own skin.

You woke hungover in a tavern that stank of the night before, Heimdall long gone, Varka already rifling sleeping pockets for unpaid tabs. The elves had not been welcome at the Midsommerblót, and that was the last straw. While the city slept off its mead, the Eastern Shallows broke open.

It started at the distribution centre, where the orks hand out rot to starving elves. A cloaked elf hobbled in muttering the same elvish over and over, then dropped his robe to show bloodied runes carved head to foot and glowing blue, spat one final word, and went up in a blast of blue fire that took the guards with him. By the time you were on your feet the whole district was in revolt, and it was organised. Explosions at the distribution centre. Overseers strung up and mutilated near the Tattered Loom. Elves swarming the Weeping Veins. If you did nothing, the Pact would answer with extermination.

You went to Veylith. Past the burning, past the Ember Chain forge where enslaved elven smiths still screamed, you found him, and you chose the elves' side. You agreed to carry their demands to the Pact and to try to hold back the enforcers when they came. You learned their words, and learned what the rune-martyrs are: the Siraen Valar, who carve commands into their own flesh and trigger them with a word and a touch. And you learned why the city cannot simply kill them all and be done. Hammerfall runs on the Weeping Veins and on elven magic, the food and the craft that keep it fed and standing. The Norse and the Orks can take a city. They cannot run one.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The rebellion is on: the elves have risen, organised, with rune-martyrs, the Siraen Valar, willing to die in blue fire.
  • You stand with the elves: you agreed to carry their demands to the Pact and to shield them from the enforcers.
  • The demands: their own businesses in the ghettos, freedom to travel the Shallows, the right to carry and spend coin.
  • The city's leash: Hammerfall depends on elven labour in the Weeping Veins and elven magic for food and craft. That is the elves' only real leverage.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: In the burning Eastern Shallows with Veylith, the elves' demands in hand and the Pact's enforcers on the way.

docs.title: 1.4.05 - Price of rebellion
docs.text:
You went to carry words to the Pact, and the Pact answered first, with soldiers.

Before your demands could be delivered, the Pact made its own reply. Soldiers armed for war marched into the distribution centre with no parley. Elves who resisted were cut down, elves who did not were shackled and dragged off, blood in the gutters and incantations dying in elven throats. It was not lawkeeping. It was extermination, and it did not care whether you were old, young, or crippled.

So the fight for the Shallows began in earnest, street by street and block by block. You threw yourselves into the Ember Chain and got every last elf out of the Pact's grip, families pulled back together out of the slaughter. Three of the saved stood out, and others began to rally to them: Aelion Varethil, a weathered war-commander; Theren Silquar, calm and arcane-blooded; and Vaelen Duskthorn, fire-eyed, carrying the will of the gods. The map was grim. For every block you reclaimed the Pact took two. But the elves had banners to stand under now.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The Pact chose extermination: no negotiation, just soldiers. Every elf is the enemy to them, regardless of age.
  • Three leaders saved: Aelion Varethil (war-commander), Theren Silquar (arcane), Vaelen Duskthorn (Siraen Valar devout). The elves are rallying behind them.
  • A war of inches: you can win ground, but the Pact takes more. This will not be won in a single push.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Holding a foothold in the Shallows with the elves rallied behind three rescued leaders, the Pact pressing on every side.

docs.title: 1.4.06 - Tug of War
docs.text:
The war became a contest of hilltops, and you took the one that mattered with a horn and a hard fight.

In the elven war council, Aelion, Theren, and Vaelen argued the next move: press the Pact while you still had surprise, or fortify and let more elves flee to safety behind your lines. With their people lending strength to your efforts, you went for the throat of the terrain. The crossroads on the plateau in the middle of the Shallows, high ground that would let whoever held it stage and defend.

You took it. The Sentinel Horn sang false threats across the district and pulled Pact skirmishers off in the wrong direction, and you charged the line and broke it. The fight was close, blade on shield and rune on steel, but you held the hill, and dozens more elves slipped past the Pact's grip to your sanctuary while you did. Somewhere in the middle of it, at long last, Hanzt shook the filth fever for good.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The crossroads is yours: you hold the strategic high ground in the Shallows now, which makes taking the rest easier.
  • The Sentinel Horn earns its keep: you used it to misdirect the Pact and win the hill.
  • Hanzt is cured: the filth fever is finally gone.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Dug in on the crossroads plateau, the elven sanctuary growing behind your lines, the Pact regrouping.

docs.title: 1.4.07 - Drawn lines
docs.text:
The elves smuggled you under the city and out behind the Pact's lines, with three commanders to kill before the runners got back.

The hill had bought time, not safety. The Pact kept grinding deeper, two blocks gained for every one you took, their numbers seemingly without end. So you stopped trying to match them block for block and went for the head instead. The elves used the catacombs to slip you behind enemy lines, up into the heart of the Elven Ghetto where the Pact's force was camped, three divisions still split along the old lines of race and clan, their commanders' tent pitched in the centre. Take the commanders and the whole force comes apart.

They were proud and they underestimated you, and they did not run. You struck the command tent and broke them before runners could bring reinforcements. It cost the elves the ghetto itself in the end, but the blow landed. The Pact's advance stalled, their next push blunted, their momentum bled out. You came away the richer for it too, gemstones off the dead.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• You decapitated a Pact force: the commanders in the Elven Ghetto are dead, and the Pact's advance has stalled.
  • The cost: the ghetto fell even so. This is still a war of trades.
  • The catacombs are a back door: the elves can move you under the city, behind the Pact's lines.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Pulling back from the Elven Ghetto with the Pact's commanders dead and their advance stalled, the war still undecided.

docs.title: 1.4.08 - Convening
docs.text:
You held a ring of broken stone for ten desperate rounds, and the elven dead came up out of the ground to finish the Pact for you.

With the Pact's commanders dead, the elves were split on what came next. Aelion wanted to strike now, while the warcamps were leaderless. Theren wanted something older. Heimdall's Temple still stood, and within it the devout could wake the spirits of the elven dead who had bled into this ground for generations. You backed Theren. The ritual needed time, and time meant holding the temple while the Pact threw everything it had at the casters.

You held. Wave after wave came, rabble first, then veterans, then a local commander, and you kept the casters alive through all ten rounds of it by the narrowest margin. Then the chant peaked, the ground shook, and ghostly hands wreathed in pale green flame clawed up out of the stone. Ancestral elves in ceremonial war-garb, eyes hollow and hungry, drove themselves into the Pact soldiers one by one. The possessed dropped their weapons, dropped to their knees, and fell dead with black ichor pooling beneath them. Not one Pact soldier was left standing. Then the spirits lifted their hollow eyes and unravelled back beyond the veil, and it was quiet.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The ancestors answered: the ritual at Heimdall's Temple woke the elven dead, who wiped out the Pact across the temple and the districts around it.
  • A horror remembered: word of what was done here, the possessed dying with black ichor in their mouths, will spread. This is a power the city will not forget.
  • The tide turns: the elves now hold real ground, and the Pact has been bloodied badly.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Standing in Heimdall's Temple among the Pact dead and the fading spirits, the rebellion suddenly winning.

docs.title: 1.4.09 - Gor'mak Thruk
docs.text:
The Pact's soldiers were sellswords, not zealots, so you walked into their camp to ask them what their lives were worth.

The temple still stank of burned flesh and black ichor. The Pact had broken and fallen back to two strongholds, and the people holding them were not believers. They were veterans and thrill-seekers who had been promised easy pay and easy prey, and the prey had turned out to have teeth. Whispers ran their ranks. Is this fight worth the coin? Worth our lives?

So you went to them rather than through them. Into the warcamp, under the eyes of dozens of fighters, to deal with Korran the Bloodred, an old scarred ork leading on respect alone, and Braya Skullveil, a human duelist who hated elves but could read the wind. These were people who answered to strength and reputation, not pity, and you spoke their language. Better to settle this with one honest clash than to keep burying each other in the mud for a city that would forget their names. You put the war itself on the line as a single reckoning, a Gor'mak Thruk, rather than another season of slaughter.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The Pact's soldiers are mercenaries: with their officers dead, they fight for coin and habit, not cause. They can be turned.
  • Korran the Bloodred and Braya Skullveil: the two holding the broken warhorde together. Strength and reputation move them, not mercy.
  • A clash to end it: rather than grind on, you pushed to settle the war in a single sanctioned confrontation.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: In the Pact's warcamp, having steered the sellswords toward ending the war by one decisive clash instead of endless butchery.

docs.title: 1.4.10 - Audience
docs.text:
The elves mustered in full to force the Pact to the table, and the ground chose that moment to open onto Hel.

The time for whispers was over. Veylith called every elf to the field, banners up in defiance, the full strength of a people refusing to be erased. This was not conquest. It was survival, a show of force to drag the Pact from extermination to the negotiating table. The host split into three strikes under Aelion, Theren, and Vaelen, with Veylith steeling the ranks, and you threw your weight behind the push on the Pact's strongholds and their leaders.

Then the ground betrayed everyone. A deep rumble, a force that dragged you toward the earth and pinned your limbs, and then a thunderclap from below as the battlefield erupted in a geyser of rock and sickly green and purple vapour. Claws too long and a slick serpent tongue tore their way out of the wound in the world. Things half-formed and dripping ichor began hauling themselves up from Hel itself, into the middle of your war. Hammerfall was not just a battlefield any more. It was a gate.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The elves went all in: Veylith mustered the whole host to force the Pact to negotiate rather than be wiped out.
  • A gate to Hel: something tore open under the battlefield and began pouring monsters into the city, mid-confrontation.
  • War on hold: elf and Pact alike now have a far worse problem clawing up out of the ground.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: On a battlefield split open by a rift to Hel, the war with the Pact suddenly the smaller of two emergencies.

docs.title: 1.4.11 - A new challenger approaches
docs.text:
Hel's brood poured into the city, and for once elf and Pact lowered their blades at each other to raise them at something worse.

The rift kept giving. Pale elongated limbs and scything claws, jaws that unhinged too wide, some slick with ichor and some plated in clattering carapace. Worst were the ones that wore a trace of the familiar: twisted mockeries of elf-kind, faces stretched and broken, ears drawn into cruel spines, eyes gone to hollow voids. For a heartbeat the whole battlefield simply stared, elf and Pact alike, every blade lowered in the raw animal understanding that something older and fouler than their quarrel had arrived.

Then it came in droves, and the line between enemy and ally dissolved. Pact veterans and elven host found themselves back to back against Hel's children, and you were in the thick of it, cutting the nightmare down as it clawed for Hammerfall. The common horror did what no parley could. It put the war down, at least for tonight.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Hel invaded Hammerfall: monsters poured from the rift, including grotesque, corrupted echoes of elves.
  • An accidental truce: facing the brood, the Pact and the elves fought side by side. The thing that paused the war was a worse enemy.
  • The wound remains: the gate to Hel under the city is the real story now.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Holding the line beside former enemies against Hel's brood, the elven war drowned out by something far worse coming up from below.

docs.title: 1.5.1 - The Pact
docs.text:
You climbed to the marble heart of the city to be judged by the men who rule it, and the night ended with a woman dragged screaming into the Pit.

The fighting done and an uneasy truce holding, you marched up into Upper Hammerfall, the old elven seat, where the streets turn to marble and wildflower and impossible grace even under occupation. In the council chamber, where many seats once sat, only three were filled: Orgrim Doomhammer, chieftain of all the Orks, vast and still in his prime with the skull-faced doomhammer across his knees; Trader Yennen, fat and conniving and plainly the Guild's creature; and the Speaker of the Chosen, robed and silent. Jarl Ivar took the fourth seat, an old moustachioed cretin. Here the fate of the Hammerfall elves would be settled, with Veylith at your side. He thanked you with his whole heart, and with gifts: elven boots and a deathdrinking rune for Gunnar, an elven cloak for Riven, a walking cauldron and a parting blade for Hanzt.

Then it soured. Ivar came home drunk to his wife Ygritte, threw filth at her, and when she slapped him he knocked her to the floor in front of their son. He stormed off to his brutes and his drink. Hours later the night broke open with smoke and screaming. The long-limbed things from the Pit came up into the streets, and they were not here to fight. They were here to take. They dragged villagers off toward the wound in the earth, and among them, a flash of crimson and a scream of fury, Ygritte, hauled down into the Pit and swallowed.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The Pact's faces: Orgrim Doomhammer (Orks), Trader Yennen (the Guild's man), the Speaker of the Chosen, and Jarl Ivar (Norse). This is who rules Hammerfall.
  • Ygritte taken: Ivar's wife, and Balder's stolen bride, dragged into the Pit by the creatures. Balder told you the whole bitter story.
  • You are going down: you formed up to follow the captors into the Pit, first into the dark after Ygritte.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: At the mouth of the Pit in Upper Hammerfall, about to descend after Ygritte and her captors.

docs.title: 1.6.1 - Into the dark
docs.text:
You went down after Ygritte's captors and found a name the elves had wiped out of their own royal line.

The Pit took you down into the bones of the catacombs after the Blood Elves and their stolen people. A drained well stood at the centre of the first chamber, its lever waiting inside the door. Deeper in, you found the Speaker already there, unhurried, before a great mural of the Thrandurassil family tree, the royal line of the elves laid out in stone and gold.

Something on it had been hidden. Under Queen Aduni's line, where a name should sit beside the High King Caerdan, the stone had been smoothed over with magic. You stripped the concealment away, and there she was: Hannah, daughter of Aduni, sister to the king, struck from her own family's memory. The Speaker watched as though he had always known the gap was there. The girl from the portal, Heimdall's lost daughter, had been royalty too, and someone had gone to great trouble to erase her.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Hannah's blood: the mural names her a daughter of Queen Aduni and sister to High King Caerdan, deliberately erased from the royal line by magic.
  • The Speaker is here: down in the catacombs ahead of you, seeming to know more than he says.
  • Deeper still: the chamber opens onward into the dark, Ygritte's captors somewhere ahead.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Deep in the catacombs at the Thrandurassil mural, Hannah's erased name uncovered, the Speaker watching and the captors still ahead.

docs.title: 1.6.2 - Tomb of the Lost
docs.text:
The Speaker sealed the way home behind you, and left you to find an empty tomb with Hannah's name on it.

The stone ground shut behind you, the Speaker's hollow face the last thing you saw before the barrier slid home. No way back. You stood in the Tomb of the Lost, walls weeping cold, and pressed on because forward was the only direction left.

A door locked itself at your backs as you went, sealing the Thrandurassil tomb for good. Ahead, a great central pyramid held the tombs and statues of Queen Aduni and King Hadran. Atop it, one of the Chosen was waiting with a pet demon, and at his word the dormant Blood Elves stirred to life around you. You fought across the levels of it, through darkness and dispelled darkness, and put them down, though the demon slipped away into the black. Ygritte's captors had already gone deeper and lost you. But two things in that chamber mattered more than the fight. Moving Aduni's statue uncovered a hidden hatch below. And set in the wall stood a vast sealed door carved with the Pilgrimage of the Eight, eight god-symbols missing from its face. Beyond a secret passage you found Hannah's own tomb, and her sarcophagus was empty.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• No way back: the Speaker sealed you into the catacombs. The only road is deeper.
  • The sealed door: a great door carved with the Pilgrimage of the Eight, missing eight divine symbols. It will not open without them.
  • Hannah's empty tomb: her sarcophagus is empty. Whatever she is now, she did not stay buried.
  • The demon escaped: the Chosen's babau slipped away, and the captors are still ahead with Ygritte.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Sealed in the Tomb of the Lost past the royal pyramid, an empty tomb and a door of eight missing runes ahead of you.

docs.title: 1.6.3 - Pilgrimage of the Eight
docs.text:
A dead girl's empty tomb and a door of eight runes gave your wandering a name and a purpose at last.

You came up out of the catacombs bloodied, the Chosen and his demon dead behind you, Hannah's empty tomb and that vast eight-runed door fresh in your minds. The council gave you little peace. When you named the Chosen's treachery, the Speaker denied all of it, cold and smooth, calling the dead one a cursed defector who shamed his order. But Veylith stepped forward with a memory: the Pilgrimage of the Eight, an old elven rite through eight temples buried beneath the Shallows, shrines that once bound an ancient evil with the power of the gods themselves. And Grukk One-Tusk produced two runes he had found securing the catacombs, faintly glowing with divine power. The legend was real, and more runes were waiting.

So down you went again, this time with purpose. The first temple was Frigg's, all family and fate. A mystral orb let you step into the temple's own glorious past as a living memory, where you met a clever young spider that the elves of this place had once loved and kept. But the present held the Weaver, that same spider grown monstrous, abandoned by the Thrandurassil elves when the Blood Elves tore this place apart and left to survive alone on scraps of prophecy and planar tricks. She blamed the elves who left her, and she was not wrong to. You put her down and took the Rune of Frigg. Beyond, the waters ran pure and blue again, and across a footbridge stood the temple of Njord.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The Pilgrimage of the Eight: collect the eight divine runes to open the sealed door. It once bound an ancient evil. Veylith named it; the door you found is its lock.
  • Three in hand: Grukk's two runes plus the Rune of Frigg. Five temples to go.
  • The Weaver's grievance: the elves abandoned her to the Blood Elves. Not every monster down here started as one.
  • Njord is next: the way to his temple is open across the water.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: On the footbridge to Njord's temple, the Rune of Frigg claimed and the Pilgrimage finally understood.

docs.title: 1.6.4 - Temple of Njord
docs.text:
Njord's temple was a drowned and stagnant thing, and it did not want you stirring its waters.

The warmth of Frigg's memory faded behind you into the cold mist of Njord's cavern. Once this lake had mirrored the sea above. Now it lay black and stinking, algae drifting like green veins, the sunken temple a faint glimmer far below. The water nymph's gift sat cool in your lungs, and you went down into it.

At the lake's floor a great stone gate carved with Njord's waves and trade ships barred the way, three of his priests' statues floating nearby, each stripped of its holy shell. You found the shells and set them back, and the current calmed in sign of the Sea-Father's approval, the gate grinding open. Deeper in waited half-flooded corridors and mirror-bright air pockets, and a whispering current with a price: to raise the sea's favour, still your storms within. You brought calm to the drowned halls rather than violence, and the raging water settled into a passable stair leading down toward Njord's heart.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Njord's temple is drowned and corrupted: its once-pure waters have gone stagnant and hostile.
  • It rewards reverence: restoring the priests' shells and calming the waters opened the way. Force only angers the tide.
  • Deeper still: the path to Njord's heart, and his rune, lies below.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Descending the drowned stair into the heart of Njord's temple, the way calmed but the corruption not yet faced.

docs.title: 1.6.5 - Temple of Njord depths
docs.text:
You raised a drowned god's temple, and then the Speaker pulled back his hood and told you that you had been his tools all along.

The temple was failing as you moved through it, your very presence cracking the old magic, water roaring in through new fissures. You cut through the sea-devils and the dimming commerce-runes, where gold turned out to be rusted iron and barnacle, then through a hall where the undead of three mad priests had been killing each other for centuries. At the bottom waited the Drowned Sanctum: three acolytes bound in seaweed chains, and above them a Spirit of Corruption, the warped soul of a fourth priest, leaping from body to body and riding each acolyte's stolen aspect in turn, current and commerce and depth. You broke the corruption loose and destroyed the spirit without letting the acolytes die, and Njord's waters ran clean again. The temple rose. At the base of his statue you took the Symbol of Njord, four of eight, and his blessing settled on you.

You carried the good news back toward the council, and that is where it broke. The Speaker was waiting, and this time he did not pretend. He had walked you through all of it. He told you the truth through one of his own. Hannah, a hard half-elf girl the highborn spat on, was promised power by a hooded servant of Osgilliath, the Lord of the Depths. She cut off her own arms on a black altar, and the pit answered, filling her with molten fire and burning claws. She died on a cross and rose as Hannah the Forsaken, and the blood she fed her followers made the Blood Elves. For centuries they have served her, waiting. And the prison she had been kept in was the very portal you shattered at the start of all this. You freed her soul. You returned her to Osgilliath. All that was left to find was a host of Heimdall's line strong enough to hold her, and the Speaker's eyes went to Ivar. Then he raised hooked claws and demonic limbs out of his robes, his patience finally spent, and the Blood Elves fell on Hammerfall in force.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Njord's rune claimed: four of the eight, and Njord's blessing with it.
  • Hannah the Forsaken: a half-elf who gave herself to Osgilliath, Lord of the Depths, and rose a monster. The Blood Elves drink her blood. This is who you are racing to stop.
  • You were used: the girl you freed from the portal at the very beginning was Hannah's imprisoned soul. Breaking that ruby played straight into the Speaker's plan.
  • A host of Heimdall's line: the resurrection needs one. The Speaker's eyes fell on Ivar.
  • The Speaker is no man: he revealed himself a clawed, demonic thing, and Hammerfall is under all-out Blood Elf attack.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Njord's rune in hand and the Speaker unmasked, Hammerfall erupting into a Blood Elf assault with Hannah's resurrection now in motion.

docs.title: 1.7.1 - Rethra kael
docs.text:
The Speaker named his plan out loud, the Blood Elves came through the doors, and a son settled a debt with his father.

The council chamber became a slaughter. The Speaker took his place and spoke of long plans and demons and a name, Hannah, and of drinking at her blood altar until the voice in the deep spoke back. Soon she would rise again, he said, now that a host had been found, and his eyes went to Ivar. Then clawed hands shoved the doors wide and the Blood Elves poured in, festering and swollen and still half-elven in the face, and every faction in the hall fought its own desperate corner.

The humans were in trouble fast. Ivar was not the warrior he had been, and he went down wounded and bleeding and drenched in his own piss, crying for help. Balder, who had every reason to let him die, led you to him instead, and Gunnar struck the killing blow on the beast standing over him. Then Balder put his blade to Ivar's throat and found he could not do it. I should put you down for everything you have done, he said, but I am not a strong enough man, and I will not hold you guilty for my own weakness. He turned away. And a blade came through Ivar's chest from behind, clean and expert. Harald stood over him wiping the steel, his voice shaking. You are not my father. Then his hatred turned to the Speaker.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The Speaker's plan, spoken aloud: Hannah the Forsaken will rise again, and a host has been chosen.
  • Ivar is dead: spared by Balder, killed by Harald, who named him no father of his.
  • Blood Painters: the Blood Elf horde came with them, and the whole council fought through the night to hold the chamber.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: In the broken council chamber, Ivar dead and Harald's wrath turned on the Speaker, the Blood Elves still coming.

docs.title: 1.7.2 - Her Speaker
docs.text:
You killed the Speaker and read his plans off his corpse, and learned exactly how little time was left.

You stood with Harald and Orgrim, Grukk One-Tusk and Yennen against the Speaker and his horde, and at the end of it the Speaker lay dead. On his body was a book bound in claw and strange symbols, and what you could piece from it told the whole shape of the thing in five fragments. A timeline: five nights until convergence, midnight, an alignment with Hannah's original ascension, no delay possible. Requirements: a vessel of Heimdall's line secured, blood still harvested nightly, temples of blood recovered, alphas reconstituted. A ritual site: a deep chamber below Upper Hammerfall, a black stone altar among root-veins, sealed by the Pilgrimage of the Eight. Instructions: keep the surface afraid, capture the weak rather than kill them, prepare the final offerings on the fifth night. And a last line in another hand. She remembers the first time. The soul is not to interfere. The Forsaken will be reborn.

You barred the great doors and endured to dawn. When you opened them the city was a graveyard, and worse than the dead were the missing, taken below. In daylight the Pact simply dissolved. Norse to Norse, Orks to their kin, elves to the mines, the Guild to its vaults, and the city began to bleed out as people fled the nightly horrors. Balder left with Harald to steward the Norse and keep a boy-jarl's people from scattering. The rest of you turned to the only thing that mattered: the eight runes, the sealed door, and the ritual you now had five nights to stop.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The Speaker is dead, and his notes are yours. Five nights to midnight, when Hannah is reborn.
  • The ritual site: a chamber below Upper Hammerfall behind the Pilgrimage door. The eight runes are the only way in.
  • The Pact is finished: every faction has scattered to its own. The city is on its own at night.
  • Balder is gone for now: steward to Harald and the Norse.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Alone with the task as the Pact crumbles, five nights to gather the runes and stop Hannah's rebirth.

docs.title: 1.7.3 - Afternight
docs.text:
To open a temple of justice you had to bleed the right blood, and Gunnar's was just elven enough.

You came up to level with the danger, hardened by the night you survived. The truth was out now: the Chosen of Osgilliath, the nightly tide of creatures from the catacombs, the weak dragged below, five days until Hannah rises again at midnight under the same sky that made her five centuries ago. The city did not know. You did. So you went back down for the runes, starting with Tyr.

The Temple of Tyr would not open for just anyone. A scale set in a seamless wall, a blood-drain beneath it, and worn elvish around it: not all blood weighs true, only the first children may tip the balance. Gunnar gave his half-elven blood, and Loki murmured in his ear about which half the stone would remember, the scale flickering cracked for a moment before the wall opened. Inside lay the Hall of Measured Wrongs, relief after relief of elven law in stone. An elf shamed for taking an orc to bed. Children beaten for letting lower races into their game. A hunter stripped for kindness to Norsemen. An orc starved to death in living roots for crossing a boundary. An elf branded for trading with humans. Then one relief stepped out of the wall as spectres and made you its court, each of you cast as accuser, defender, witness, or judge, with no say in the role. You ruled as the elves would have ruled. You upheld their law and their cruelty, and the cold hall accepted you and opened the way on.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Level 7, and the clock is loud: five nights until the ritual, the Chosen revealed, the city under nightly siege.
  • Gunnar's blood is a key: the Temple of Tyr opened only for elven blood. His half answered. Loki found it very funny.
  • You chose elven law: in the Hall of Measured Wrongs you upheld the elves' brutal justice rather than temper or reject it. The hall marked you as aligned with it.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Inside the Temple of Tyr, past the Hall of Measured Wrongs, the first of the night's runes still ahead and the ritual days running out.

docs.title: 1.7.4 - Temple of Tyr
docs.text:
Tyr's temple weighed you on a scale, then loosed a chained wolf to see what you were made of.

Past the Hall of Measured Wrongs lay the Twin Scales. A great stone balance fused into the floor, two orbs drifting in mirrored symmetry, one red and one blue, and a low patient hum that did not care how long you took.
