The red orb yielded only to a struck blow, the blue only to a spell or a clever trick, and the scale at the heart of the room punished any imbalance, crushing weight on the mind or a dragging slowness in the limbs. You learned its rhythm and held the balance long enough to pass.

The next door opened on a vast elven amphitheatre, perfect tiered stone around a central platform, and on the platform a great shape bound in chains with its head bowed. For a moment, nothing. Then a deep crack, the chains snapping taut, and the thing tore itself free, fur and fang bursting out of rigid stone into something massive and feral. A wolf. The immaculate floor split apart into safe ground and ruin as it came at you, the law of the place itself shifting underfoot from one breath to the next.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The Twin Scales: Tyr's temple tests balance, force and finesse in equal measure, or the scale makes you pay.
  • Tyr's Judgment: the chained wolf, the temple's guardian, has broken loose. The arena floor itself turns against whoever stands still.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: On the cracking floor of Tyr's amphitheatre, the Judgment loosed and the fight just begun.

docs.title: 1.7.5 - Sacrifice
docs.text:
Tyr does not weigh the gift. He weighs the giving. And he asked each of you for something you could never have back.

The wolf crashed to the stone at last, the violence draining out of it, the chains pulling it back into place at the centre of the platform. Its eyes dimmed to a low, steady light. Still. Watching. Only with the fight over did you see the five reliefs that had been on the walls the whole time. A rich man offering a coin that cost him nothing, refused. A soldier giving up his spare sword while keeping the other, refused. A warrior placing his sword-hand willingly into the jaw and fighting on with a fist of chain. A figure laying down their last weapon to stand empty-handed, and the wolf's carved eyes brighter for them than for anyone. Tyr does not weigh the gift. He weighs the giving.

Then the wolf's jaw unlocked and opened wider than any throat, and inside, on a pedestal ringed with the corroded offerings of those who came before, lay the Rune of Tyr. Beneath it, a question. What do you give willingly, that cannot be returned? Brawn put his hand into the jaw, and it closed clean and opened again, and where his hand had been was living chain-link, pale silver and warm and his to command. Riven laid down Winter's Vigil, Etta's bow, the last thing he had of someone who died for him, and Tyr gave back a bow covered in runes worked into the grain as though they had grown there. A small grey scale, smooth and warm and permanent, marked the back of every hand that gave. Tyr had seen what they offered. Five of eight.

The way down continued past the Depth 3 landing, through an iron door sealed from below five hundred years ago and never reopened. Below it the stone changed. Confident elven cuts gave way to shorter, less certain strokes, then to raw wet cave. Old water. Iron. A pressure plate sprung long ago, its echo coming back from three directions. Circuit diagrams and danger markers scratched on every surface, one symbol repeating: a sealed door with something pressing hard against it from the far side. A workbench of tiny warm tools, recently used. And from the far passage something large and deliberate began to move, stone plates and metal fittings grinding, filling the corridor. It did not rush. It stopped. It waited.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Tyr's Mark: Brawn gave his hand and bears a living chain-link limb now. Riven gave Etta's bow and carries a rune-covered bow of Tyr in its place. Each who sacrificed wears a grey scale of Tyr on the back of the hand.
  • Five of eight: the Rune of Tyr is claimed.
  • Depth 4: below the elven work lies cruder, newer construction. Kobold tools, traps, and warnings, and one recurring sign of a door holding something back.
  • Something waits: a large, maintained thing is moving in the dark ahead.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Down on Depth 4 for the first time, in kobold-built dark, something heavy waiting in the passage ahead.

docs.title: 1.8.1 - Sakapatate
docs.text:
You cracked open a machine that five hundred years of kobolds built to keep the dark at bay. Two of them were still inside.

The shoulder joint blew out at half-health. A stunned kobold tumbled free, hit the stone, took one look at you, and ran. The Sakapatate crashed sideways and lay there grinding, something still trying to work inside it, and from within came two small voices repeating the same words over and over in Draconic. Hantz could translate most of it. It was mostly profanity.

Two pilots left in there, and you had to find them. The opposite shoulder was the obvious guess. Tap on the metal, listen. You heard a kobold swear, then levers, whirring mechanisms, more swearing. You cracked it open and told them you were not going to hurt them.

One came out running, straight to the machine, already making observations and sketching damage diagrams, completely absorbed. The other came out watching you. Quiet. Measuring. Not-rotten. Not-blood-smell. Not shadow-thing. Eventually the watching stopped and the talking started.

A few minutes later an older kobold arrived from the tunnel, staff in hand, sent by the matriarch. You were to be escorted to Vress. You passed the Red Corridor on the way and asked about it. Something Hantz said landed well, and the messenger stopped, looked at you, and told you what he knew. Something bad behind the seal, getting worse, three of theirs taken in the last month. Vress knew all the histories. He did not. He picked up the pace after that.

The settlement was a grand elven chamber, vaulted arches thirty feet high, started and abandoned mid-build five centuries ago. Rope bridges between platforms, small forges along one wall, machines in every stage of completion. Sixty kobolds, maybe more, who all stopped what they were doing when you walked in. And at the far end, another Sakapatate, dormant and enormous, its stone surface carved edge to edge with diagrams and pictures, history and engineering both at once. The kobolds' most important record and their ultimate weapon, the same object.

Vress sat elevated at the far end. Grey-scaled. One eye milky and sightless. Three fingers gone from her left hand. Skit stood at her shoulder, young and quick, translating. The kobolds have been here since the Great Betrayal, Vress said, their name for the day everything changed. When the elves left, the kobolds stayed. They finished what they could. They sealed what they feared. They have spent four centuries asking what the shrines were built for and getting no answer.

Hantz stayed long enough with the engineers that they started showing him things unprompted. Brawn's chain hand drew a crowd, careful fingers working the joints. Riven's bow drew looks. Then you asked to see the temples, and the kobolds led you deeper.

Past the settlement, into the passage where elven stonework gives out and raw cave takes over, to a plain room. Kobold crates against the walls, nothing obviously holy. A rope hanging from the ceiling. Two empty stone perches on opposite walls. Ravens carved into every surface, each facing a different direction. Gunnar grabbed the rope and hung from it, and closed one eye. The floor opened. What hit him was not a voice but knowledge, the weight of it pressing in all at once, the shape of something half-known made whole. Odin's presence. Not a gift. An acknowledgement. The staircase descended into the dark below the altar, grinding stone echoing back from both directions. The sanctum was waiting.

"The cost of knowing is always the same. What you see with both eyes is the world as others show it to you. What you see with one is the world as it is."

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Gunnar, Odin's mark: something settled into him when the floor opened. It will not last long.
  • Four nights left: you have not slept since the catacombs began. The clock is still running.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: The staircase is open. Below is Odin's sanctum, and the rune. Two still remain after that.

docs.title: 1.8.2 - Odin
docs.text:
You came down here for a rune. You left with six, a handful of dead gods' words, and a path back up into Guild territory.

You descended into Odin's sanctum and found, for the first time on this level, a room the elves had actually finished. Clean stone. No tool marks. No salvage. No kobold repair. The raven puzzle gave way under Gunnar's knowledge of Huginn and Muninn, and the hidden cache opened with it. Four rune-scrolls waited inside. Two yielded their names quickly: Read Omens and Talking Corpse. The other two kept their secrets. When you climbed back out, a gaggle of young kobolds had already gathered at the threshold, peering into the exposed sanctum like children staring into a myth that had just become real. Vress and Skit were there too, watching the room that had stayed closed for five centuries finally answer someone.

Back in the Matriarch's Hall, you stayed. You rested. You explored. The kobolds stopped being a strange people in the dark and became, for a little while, a city with merchants, gossip, tools, and curiosity. Hantz found plenty to interest him, especially among the wares and half-finished ideas of the engineers below. Before you left, Vress showed you the objects recovered from the sealed flower-shrine: an ivory comb carved with interlocking flowers, a sealed glass vial, and three hollow coins marked in old elven script. Hantz's forensics turned the vial into something human-sized again: perfume, or cologne, smelling of flowers and sugarcane. Then memory landed. These coins would never have survived into Guild circulation. And in Richeri, among the Guild's holdings, there was a place called the Hollow Coin.

You took that clue to the smooth stone wall of Freya's sealed shrine and found exactly nothing except confirmation. The flower symbol above. The perfect stone. No seam. No mechanism. No way through from this side. So you turned east and entered the halls you now believed belonged to Thor.

The first room had warnings. The second had old machinery and a portcullis the kobolds had never truly solved. Brawn did what Brawn does: grabbed the broken chain and hauled. For a moment the gate rose. For a moment it looked simple. Then it slammed down again and crushed Gunnar's first attempt to wedge it open. Hantz adapted faster. Metal spike. Sluice gate. Better leverage. Brawn hauled a second time and this time the old mechanism held. Beyond it waited the flooded chamber with its strange magnetic pillars. Gunnar went first again, brave enough to test the center plate with his foot. Nothing. Then he stepped onto it fully.

The water surged.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Odin's sanctum: You claimed the Odin rune and found four rune-scrolls. Two are identified: Read Omens and Talking Corpse.
  • Vress's recovered objects: The comb, perfume vial, and hollow coins point toward the Hollow Coin in Richeri as the likely way into Freya's shrine.
  • The long rest: You slept in the kobold settlement. The ritual clock moved again.
  • Thor's temple: You got through the machinery hall and into the flooded chamber. The room has now begun to answer you.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: You are standing in Thor's flooded chamber beneath Hammerfall. The water has surged, the room has woken, and whatever waits in the storm is about to rise.

docs.title: 1.8.3 - The Storm
docs.text:
You broke the storm, claimed Thor's rune, and came back up into a city that had already started to splinter.

You finished Thor's trial, though it tested your limits. Three chains joined to the plate, the arena drained, the Storm Warden letting go of its duty with the trial fulfilled. Brawn's enormous strength hauled each chain into place, aided by Gunnar's voluptuous thighs. The level 3 wand of Heal burned itself out saving you when the fight could have gone the other way. When the water finally drained, Thor's rune waited below the storm-slick stone. Seven of eight now. One left.

Back with the kobolds, you returned the mechanism and the amulet. Six engineers swarmed Hantz's crossbow and rebuilt it around a storm battery and blue water taken from the storm itself. The weapon came back changed, no longer a bolt-thrower but something stranger. Vress took the amulet in both hands and told you it had belonged to her ancestor and former Matriarch, Vrakka, who once worked alongside the builders before they fled the tunnels. Skit nearly bounced out of his skin when he saw the mechanism, then rushed you to the engineers so they could all talk over one another in their excitement. When you left, Vress held out a hand and said:

"Three of ours. Last week. Red Below has been moving closer."

You came up at eight on a still morning and found the Shallows broken. The Eastern Elf Shallows was a ghost town. The Western was being emptied into the mines every night. Veylith said the Blood Elf attacks were forcing them underground, and that Hammerfall itself was splintering. The Norse were breaking apart after Ivar's death. The Orks were retreating inward, trusting nothing but their old traditions. The Guild was still standing, still armed, still making sure the people who could pay had safety. The people who could not fled before nightfall and the darkness that came for them.

When you asked about the Hollow Coins, Theren Silquar gave you the old answer. Freya. Life. Fertility. Love. New beginnings. He remembered the spring rites in Dres Elas, when the elven noble families gathered to offer willing sacrifices and ask Freya for children and blessing. The place you know as the Hollow Coin had once been something else entirely. A temple. A place of love and lust, of blood given freely. By the end, the tavern in Richeri was no longer a rumour. It was a lead to the last thing standing between you and Hannah.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Thor's rune is claimed: the party now holds seven of eight.
  • Hantz's crossbow: the kobold mechanism went back where it belonged, and his weapon came back rebuilt around the storm.
  • Vrakka: Vress's ancestor and former Matriarch. Returning her amulet hit the old kobold hard.
  • The Chosen are winning: the Shallows is a nightly horror and Hammerfall splinters further each night. They keep the surface afraid and capture the weak, readying the final offerings.
  • The Hollow Coin: not just a tavern. It was once a place of Freya herself, and it is your last stop before Hannah.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Headed for Richeri and the Hollow Coin, almost holding everything you need to stop her.

docs.title: 1.8.4 - The Hollow Coin
docs.text:
You spent the night losing money, winning fights, and negotiating your way through a woman who could read a guild coin at twenty paces. By morning you were standing in a room where two dead men had already tried what you were about to try.

The Hollow Coin earned its reputation. The fighting pit pulled Brawn in within the first hour. He beat Dok, the young Ork at ringside who'd lost his own fight and was watching to learn something, then stepped back in against Egil, the veteran, and lost. The crowd had bet on him both times. Riven took a seat at the Raven's Cross tables, won his first two hands cleanly, then spent the rest of the evening cheating with perfect invisibility and losing anyway. Gunnar went upstairs after the lightest shove of peer pressure anyone has ever called convincing. The mission was intelligence. Information. A simple transaction. When push came to shove, Gunnar chose to shove. Erevyn, an elf three months into her time at the Hollow Coin, happily relieved him of his gold and everything else he was carrying, and somewhere in the proceedings mentioned that her mother used to call this building something else. Galanthas. The Flower Gate. She didn't know what it meant. She knew Brynja did.

Brynja found the table when she'd made up her mind about them. The pitch was simple: a space beneath the establishment, old elven construction, workers who'd gone down and hadn't come back useful. She needed capable people. They looked capable. Down the road at the Notched Axe, Ulf was three-quarters of the way through a quantity of drink he couldn't afford and had been trying for three days to explain what he'd seen to anyone who'd listen. He told you. Three of them went in. The other two rushed ahead through the arch. The circle accepted them. Ulf was the odd one out, left behind in the dark with a portal that wouldn't open and two companions who didn't come back. He wasn't going back. Gunnar left him enough coin for the rest of the evening and you walked back.

The second negotiation with Brynja went better. A hundred gold, rooms for the night, drinks. She agreed. Then mentioned, almost as an afterthought, that she knew plenty of Guild members with coins like Hantz carries, and none of them tried to hide theirs from the people around them. The negotiation ended there. You slept it off behind her walls.

In the morning Brynja took you to the cellar. Heavy door, worn flower symbol above the lintel. A room older than everything above it. The circle in the floor, fifteen feet across, three shallow depressions in the stone. Three hollow coins in hand.

You placed them.

Pale gold light. Flowers, and underneath something older. Iron. An old wound still present in the air.

The circle opened. You stepped through.

Behind you: the clean stone wall you'd spent weeks on the wrong side of. On the floor: two bodies. Not together. No visible wounds.

On the left wall, cut into the stone in dancing elven script, a story:

The Lay of Hrafn and the Hidden Lady

She was tired of being worshipped.

Ten thousand years of flowers. Of fathers on their knees before a wedding. Of mothers burning cloth when the children would not come. She knew what every face wanted before it opened its mouth. She had not been surprised in centuries. So she took off the cloak that lets a woman fly, set it down on the floor of Fólkvangr, and walked out without it.

She called herself Runa. She walked into the worst tavern she could find.

She found Hrafn.

He was winning at dice. This was suspicious, because the dice were his, and they were loaded. He had a face built for borrowing things and a laugh like a man who had never once considered consequences. She sat beside him and ordered the cheap drink.

He stole her purse before she finished it.

She let him.

Two rounds later he looked at her sideways. "You know I have your money."

"I know."

"You haven't called for anyone."

"No."

The longest pause in the history of his particular profession. "...Why?"

She said: "I wanted to see what you'd do next."

This was, for Hrafn, completely fatal. He fell in love immediately and completely and against every interest he had.

Now the gods were watching, as gods do, and some of them were not pleased. The problem with mortals, they said, is that they are always almost worthy and then they aren't. Three bets were placed.

Odin said: Watch the first road.

Three weeks into their travelling they came to a crossroads. One road had a checkpoint. Hrafn had forty-seven reasons not to walk through a checkpoint. The other road took three days longer and crossed a river that drowned men in spring.

He looked at the checkpoint. At the river. At her.

"Can you swim?"

"No."

He took the river road without another word.

She watched him from his back, her arms around his neck, the current at his chest and rising. His jaw was set. He was not asking for credit. He was not saying anything at all. He was just walking.

She thought: hm.

Odin said nothing. He paid the bet.

Thor said: Watch the second road.

They sheltered from a storm in a cave that went much deeper than it looked, and in the second hour she twisted her ankle on a root she couldn't see. Absolute dark. He could find the exit and come back, or not, and she would never know which he had chosen.

She heard him sit down.

He started talking.

He told her about the worst theft he had ever attempted and the three different ways it had gone wrong. He told her the part that still made him laugh, and then with the same voice and no pause between them, the part that didn't. He did not explain why the second part didn't. He talked for two hours.

At some point Runa understood that he was still there because he did not want her to be alone in the dark.

She had been a goddess for ten thousand years.

This was the first time anyone had sat with her in the dark without wanting anything from it.

Thor said nothing. He paid the bet.

Heimdall said: Watch the third road.

A merchant lord in the next city knew Hrafn's face and had opinions about it. He offered him the following, enumerated on fingers: land, a name, a woman of appropriate family, the forgetting of certain debts. All of it, if Hrafn delivered the foreign woman to his estate by morning.

Hrafn was quiet for long enough that the merchant lord thought it was negotiation and raised the offer.

Then Hrafn started laughing.

Not a performance. Not the laugh of a man choosing virtue loudly so someone will notice. The laugh of someone who has just understood a joke at someone else's expense. Who has seen, completely, that the merchant lord doesn't understand anything about the situation at all.

"She," he said, when he had stopped, "is the worst thing that has ever happened to my income."

He took Runa's hand. They left.

Heimdall said nothing. He paid the bet. Odin said: I told you.

She showed him what she was on the road out of the city, when it was quiet and the light was good.

He looked at her for a very long time.

"I am," he said carefully, "a significantly worse man than you deserve."

"You were. Three weeks ago you were."

He thought about this. "I still stole your purse."

"You bought me the cheap drink with it and called it even."

"That's not... I don't think that counts."

"Hrafn." She was almost smiling. "You gave it back."

After that, when the noble families came to her with their flowers and their requests, she told them she had a new requirement.

Not gold. Not ceremony. Not devotion sworn on knees.

Proof.

She built a house for it beneath the city that no longer remembers its name. She laid her requirements into the stone in the old tongue and left the entrance open and told them: those who prove worthy, I will bless. Those who do not...

On the right wall, shorter, the flourish abandoned:

You enter as one or you do not enter. Choose your partner before the threshold. Devotion is your currency and Freya will test your wealth.

Two bodies on the floor. Neither of them Ulf.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Two nights left: The party slept at the Hollow Coin. The ritual is two nights away. The temple is not a quick trip.
  • The Flower Gate is open: Three hollow coins seated, the party through. Brynja has a barrel of them, and the party kept their coins, so there is a way back.
  • Brynja's deal: 100g and rooms for clearing whatever is below. She doesn't know this is a divine shrine. She clocked Hantz's coin and didn't push. Yet.
  • Ulf: At the Notched Axe in Richeri. Three went in. The arch accepted two and locked him out. His companions didn't come back.
  • The inscription hall: Enter as a pair or don't enter. The rules are on the wall. The arch enforces them.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: You are standing in the first chamber of Freya's temple. The story is on the left wall. The rules are on the right. The arch ahead leads into the dark, and you have not yet chosen your partners.

docs.title: 1.8.5 - Devotion
docs.text:
You drew lots in the inscription hall, split into pairs, and the temple took you in two directions.

The rule carved into the wall left nothing to argue with. Enter as pairs, or do not enter. So you drew lots. Gunnar and Riven went one way, Hantz and Brawn the other, and the far arch sent each pair somewhere it could not follow. As Gunnar and Riven crossed the threshold a silver cord found them, alive and patient, and bound them wrist to wrist. It did not intend to let go.

It earned its keep almost at once. Their first trial was a flooded chamber, cold grey water moving in a slow current, a line of stones that tipped underfoot. Riven went straight in, meaning to wedge a slab steady, and the water closed over his head. He held the stone level from beneath while Gunnar picked his way across the top. Then Gunnar tried to cheat the last of it with his mirror, blinking ahead through the air, and the cord took offence. Every time he flickered free it came awake, slithered after him, and wrapped him up again with a squeeze that drove the breath out of him. They reached the far door soaked and joined and quietly bullied by a rope.

On the other side of the temple, Hantz and Brawn found a wide hall of identical tiles beneath a canopy of carved branches. Brawn learned the floor lied the fast way. A tile gave under his weight and dropped him through it. But from the walkway above, Hantz could see what the floor hid. The safe tiles caught the light differently from up there, a pattern invisible from below. He called the route down one step at a time, and Brawn walked it on faith.

Then the dark took Gunnar and Riven. Not dimness. The kind of dark that kills a torch at the threshold and gives nothing back. They felt out the first of its puzzles by voice alone, each trusting a partner he could not see. Two pairs, two roads, and the temple asking the same question in every room.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The pairs: Gunnar and Riven on one road, Hantz and Brawn on the other.
  • The cord: a living silver rope binds Gunnar and Riven. It will not let them part, and it bites when Gunnar tries to blink away from it.
  • The temple's rule: every room is built for two. Nothing in here can be done alone.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Deep in Freya's temple beneath the Hollow Coin, split into two pairs, each working through its own trials, with two nights left before midnight.

docs.title: 1.8.6 - Paired Pitfalls
docs.text:
Hantz and Brawn talked their way across a drowning room and put down a thing that would not stay hurt.

Hantz and Brawn came to a chamber split clean down the middle. A wall they could not reach across, a grid of carved rune-tiles set into the stone on either side, and above it all a great rainbow cut into the wall in dead grey stone. A small bronze plaque asked them, politely, to close the door behind them. They did. It sealed, no handle on the inner face. Somewhere deep in the wall water began to move, and the carved flowers along the floor started to weep.

A riddle ran across both halves of the room, and neither of them could read it alone. Something born where rain meets sun. Seven colours. A road strung between the hall of the gods and the fields of the living. They knew it on sight, a rainbow bridge, and the room had left help for anyone who looked: a child's painted toy with a little figure that slid up and over the arc, a shrine to a watchman with a horn, seven dull stones that fitted into the floor and shone as one. Sure of themselves, they spelled out RAINBOW. The room bit back. The water climbed a finger higher, more petals opened to pour, and the arch above stayed grey. Close. Wrong name. A moment of digging turned up the right one. Bifröst. They called the letters across the divide, one side then the other, and the bridge lit band by band as the water lapped at their ankles and rose no further.

Beyond it stood the Patient One. Smooth amber stone, taller than a man, in no hurry at all. It hit hard, and worse than that, it healed, closing its wounds nearly as fast as the two of them could open them. They found the shape of it together. It only stayed wounded while both of them kept striking, neither one hanging back to play it safe. They were convinced the lingering flames off Hantz's work were what held its healing down. Wrong about why, right enough about how, and it never came back to cost them. Nobody went down. When it finally stopped mending it did not shatter. It sat down on the flowered floor, the amber light going out of its eyes, and the way onward opened.

Two trials, and no blood owed. Hantz and Brawn came through whole. On the far side of the temple, Gunnar and Riven's reckoning is still waiting.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• Bifröst: the rainbow bridge between the realm of the gods and the world of the living. The name that opened the way.
  • The Patient One: beaten only by refusing to fight it alone. Ease off, and it healed everything back.
  • Pair A's turn: Gunnar and Riven still have a trial of their own before anyone reaches the heart of the temple.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: Hantz and Brawn have finished their side of Freya's temple. Gunnar and Riven's last trial, and whatever waits in the sanctum beyond, is still ahead.

docs.title: 1.8.7 - Freya
docs.text:
The eighth rune came home, the door drank eight gods' worth of power, and the countdown wasn't ticking toward the ritual. It was ticking toward you.

Gunnar and Riven crossed into the last chamber first. Two stone figures waited at the far end, twenty-five feet apart, facing each other, wearing the same carved face. The moment the pair crossed the threshold, the eyes opened. It went the distance. The wardens hit like falling masonry and closed their own wounds by merging into a single towering shape, a pair of eyes burning bright enough to lock a man's knees in place before the fists ever landed. Gunnar and Riven traded ground for ground with it until the last blow fell and the amber light finally guttered out.

Beyond the wardens' hall, the passage opened into the sanctum, and for the first time since the temple split them, all four stood in the same room. A fountain at the centre, water untouched in a hundred years. An altar beyond it, and on the altar, the eighth rune, warm before anyone had touched it. One by one, they put their hands to the water. Something answered. A blessing settled over both pairs, the sense of a partner's opening becoming visible the instant it appeared, the kind of thing you feel more than you're told. Then Gunnar lifted the rune off the altar and the Pilgrimage of the Eight was, at last, complete.

It was on the walk out that Riven's bow announced itself. A shot went true, clean past anything luck could explain, and the wood in his hands answered back. Winter's Vigil was gone, and had been for a while without any of them quite naming it. What Tyr gave back in its place introduced itself plainly: dark, close-grained hardwood, runes carved edge to edge without a shadow between them. Tyr's Measure, if the wood had a name to give.

They surfaced through the portal into Brynja's cellar, dusty and temple-worn, and told her only as much as they chose to. Brynja paid out a hundred gold for the trouble and gave them the run of the Hollow Coin's back rooms for the night, no charge, no questions asked past the ones she'd already swallowed. One night burned there. One remained.

The next morning they carried all eight runes down to the sealed door and set them into their places, meaning to unlock the way through before the ritual inside could finish. Power moved through the carvings like current filling a battery, ran the length of the door, and was consumed. Only then did the shape of it come clear. The door and the ritual had never been two separate things racing toward two separate ends. The eight runes that opened the way through were the exact divine power the Chosen needed to bring Hannah back, and the countdown had never been a clock on the ritual. It was the Speaker's own prediction, made months back, of the day the party would finally finish the Pilgrimage and hand it over. Unlocking the door and completing the ritual were the same act.

The stone drank the last of it and opened onto a ruin. Black corruption spread like boils across desecrated elven stonework, and huddled among it, the oldest of the Chosen and their newest converts knelt around a stone slab gone black with use. Ygritte lay across it. Dead. And rising out of her, unfolding, was something with hooked curved claws, wings like a bat's stretched wide, feet twisting into an eagle's talons, and a tail long and red tipped in black, curling up behind it like a scorpion's sting.

docs.title: Remember For Next Time
docs.text:
• The door and the ritual were the same thing. You went down to unlock the way through before the Chosen could finish. The runes that opened the door were also the divine power the ritual needed. Opening it completed it.
  • The Pilgrimage is complete. All eight runes are claimed, and the sealed door beneath Hammerfall is open, its charge already spent.
  • Freya's Blessing: a gift from the fountain, given to both pairs. Fighting alongside a partner now hits harder.
  • Tyr's Measure: the bow that came back in Winter's Vigil's place, carved edge to edge in Tyr's own runes.
  • Ygritte is dead, and something else has taken her place on the black stone.
  • The party is level 8.

docs.title: Where We Are
docs.text: The door is open, the sanctuary beyond it desecrated, and Hannah is rising in front of them, powered by the very thing they carried down to stop her. There is nowhere left to go but through this.
